profile_bio.description: We have fun here

quoted_tweet.text:
I just witnessed a Launch Edition Model Y get rear-ended, hard enough that it’s likely headed for a total loss. 😢

What makes it especially painful is that the Launch Edition came with lifetime FSD baked into the purchase price at a significant premium. Tesla made that FSD license non-transferable. So if the car is written off, the lifetime FSD dies with it. All that extra money paid upfront vanishes along with the vehicle.

This is exactly why the FSD subscription model usually makes more sense for most buyers. You pay monthly only while you own and drive the car. If it gets totaled or traded in, you simply cancel the subscription. No massive sunk cost disappears with the vehicle.

It’s a reminder that hardware is replaceable, but those non-transferable software licenses aren’t.

🚨 This headline is straight-up misleading clickbait.
“Elon Musk was paid $158 billion by Tesla last year.”
Wrong. Tesla’s own SEC filings show that’s just the grant-date accounting fair value of unvested performance-based stock awards.

This isn’t a paycheck — it’s skin in the game. He only gets rewarded if Tesla creates trillions in value for all of us shareholders.
Comparing a paper accounting number to the entire Dept of Education budget? Peak propaganda.

kept.text:
NEWS: Washington State just made the Tesla Semi $175,000 cheaper through a new rebate plan.

A fresh state initiative called WAZIP (Zero-Emission Incentive Program) went live this week, knocking up to $175,000 off the sticker price of heavy-duty electric trucks. 

The Long Range Tesla Semi made the approved list and qualifies for the full amount.

Real numbers after the rebate:
• Long Range Semi (500-mile): originally around $290,000, now drops to $115,000
• Standard Range Semi (325-mile): around $260,000, not on the approved list yet
• Average new diesel Class 8 truck: roughly $175,000

That puts the electric Semi well below what fleets pay for diesel rigs upfront, before even counting the savings on fuel and repairs down the road.

The $112 million program runs through Washington’s Climate Commitment Act and applies the discount right at checkout. This removes the biggest reason businesses have held back from going electric: the high sticker price.

The timing lines up perfectly for Tesla. The company kicked off high-volume Semi production in Nevada this week, with the plant set to build 50,000 trucks per year once running at full speed.

After years of waiting, Tesla can finally ship Semis at scale, and Washington fleet operators can grab one for less than half of what it originally cost.

Businesses can now buy and install dedicated Tesla Semi chargers, with two options designed for different use cases:

Basecharger - Built for overnight charging and longer dwell times

• 125 kW charging speed

Megacharger - Built for fast top-ups and quick stops

• 0-1000 VDC voltage range
• Up to 100m cabinet-to-post cable distance
• >96% efficiency
• ~$188,000 for a basic setup

Tesla provides over-the-air updates, monitoring and 97% uptime. Deliveries begin early 2027 as Semi production ramps up in Nevada.

This unlocks a major shift for trucking, letting depots transition to electric without major utility upgrades.

2️⃣ 베이스차저(Base Charger) 핵심 개념
테슬라가 새롭게 도입한 중량 트럭용 야간 충전 솔루션으로, 물류 운영 패턴(야간 정차)에 최적화된 설계

3️⃣ 기술적 특징
• 125kW 출력 기반 안정적 충전
• 약 6m 케이블로 유연한 연결성 확보
• 별도 AC-DC 캐비닛 없이 완전 통합 구조 → 설치 단순화

4️⃣ 비용 효율 구조
최대 3대 유닛이 하나의 차단기를 공유 → 초기 설치 비용 + 운영 비용 동시 절감

5️⃣ 경제성의 핵심 포인트
저렴한 야간 전기 요금 + 고정된 물류 루트
→ 총 소유 비용(TCO) 관점에서 디젤 대비 경쟁력 빠르게 부각

결론적으로,
세미는 단순한 차량이 아니라 “차량 + 충전 인프라 + 전력 비용 구조”가 결합된 시스템 단위로 확장되고 있음

Looks like Tesla will charge $0.08 per kWh to businesses using its chargers at revenue-generating sites. Included in that fee:

Maintenance Package:
• Annual preventative maintenance and scheduled service visits.
• Tesla technicians: experts in Semi Charging systems, with parts on hand and direct feedback loops to Engineering
• 97% uptime guarantee and 10+ year equipment service agreement (parts and labor included).

Management Software & Revenue Remittance:
• Tesla for Business with full session data, monthly usage reports, uptime insights, and pricing controls.
• Open Charge Point Interface (OCPI): Live, automated data sharing.
• Automatic revenue payouts on a set schedule, minus Tesla's fee.

Trip Planner & Dynamic Rerouting:
• Increased utilization powered by intelligent routing that considers state of charge, efficiency, site availability, and weather.
• Host-owned sites added to our Trip Planner and in-vehicle navigation, subject to the terms of the service agreement.

Network Operation, Remote Diagnostics, Driver Support:
• Network Operations Center verifying customer escalations and Semi charger disruptions 24/7/365.
• Remote detection, automatic triage, and repair, plus routine firmware updates by Tesla Service Engineering.
• Phone support for troubleshooting charging issues for all drivers (Tesla & Other).

Evolving Software Enhancing the EV Driver Experience:
• Forecasted Availability: Real-time monitoring of stall occupancy, wait times, and outages to avoid drivers being routed to full or unavailable sites.
• Down stalls and maintenance events are reflected instantly, while dynamic forecasting utilizes anonymous data to predict availability specifically at the driver's arrival time.
• Pricing: Flexible pricing tools and controls based on Tesla's decade plus in charging operations.
• Resilient Design: Plug & Charge works even without connectivity. Billing resumes automatically once reconnected.
• In-App Customer Satisfaction Score (CSAT) & Report an Issue: Drivers can rate their experience and flag issues directly in the Tesla app for faster feedback and resolution.
• And more as Tesla releases new features.

Other:
• Utility rate and tariff optimization to help you optimize energy costs.
• Internet Connectivity Management Services (includes managing and paying for the internet connection).
• Ongoing improvements and firmware updates. Chargers get better over time.

quoted_tweet.text:
A new study just blew up the entire "vibe coding" movement.

Researchers from UC San Diego and Cornell tracked 112 experienced software developers using AI agents in their actual jobs. The finding is the opposite of every viral demo on your timeline.

Professional developers don't vibe code. They control.

Here's what they actually found.

The researchers ran two studies. 13 developers were observed live as they coded with agents in real production work. 99 more answered a deep qualitative survey. Every participant had at least 3 years of professional experience. Some had 25.

The viral pitch of agentic coding goes like this. Hand the agent a vague prompt. Don't read the diff. Forget the code even exists. Trust the vibes. Andrej Karpathy coined the term. Tens of thousands of developers on X claim to run "dozens of agents at once" building entire production systems hands-off.

The data says almost nobody serious actually works that way.

Here is what experienced developers do instead.

→ They plan before they prompt. They write out the architecture, the constraints, and the edge cases first, then hand the agent a tightly scoped task.

→ They review every diff. Not because they're paranoid. Because they've seen what happens when you don't.

→ They constrain the agent's blast radius. Small, well-defined tasks only. The moment a problem touches multiple systems or has unclear requirements, they take over.

→ They treat the agent like a fast junior dev that needs supervision, not a senior engineer that can be trusted alone.

The researchers also found something darker buried in the data.

A separate randomized trial they cite showed that experienced open source maintainers were 19% slower when allowed to use AI. A different agentic system deployed in a real issue tracker had only 8% of its invocations result in a merged pull request.

92% failure rate in production. 19% productivity drop for senior devs.

The viral demos lied to you.

The paper's biggest insight is in one sentence: experienced developers feel positive about AI agents only when they remain in control. The moment they let go, quality collapses, and they know it.

This matches what every serious shop has quietly figured out. The developers shipping the most with AI right now aren't the ones vibing. They're the ones with the strictest review processes, the tightest task scoping, and the clearest mental model of what the agent can and cannot do.

Vibe coding makes for great Twitter videos.

It does not make great software.

The next time someone tells you they let Claude build their entire SaaS in a weekend, ask them how much of that code they've actually read.

The honest answer separates real engineers from the demo crowd.

kept.text:
TWO TESLA PATENTS DETAIL THE POWDER FLOW AND ROLLER GAP BEHIND ITS DRY 4680 LINE

Tesla published two patent applications on April 30 that, read together, describe the manufacturing line behind January's "both electrodes use our dry process" announcement.

US 2026/0115965 A1 and US 2026/0121013 A1 were filed seven days apart in October 2024, sat in examination for eighteen months, and emerged on April 30, just over a week after Tesla's Q1 2026 earnings call confirmed Cybercab production was underway. The two cover adjacent stations on the same calender line: powder going in, and the gap between the rolls that compresses it.

The first patent eliminates the mechanical valve in the powder dispensing apparatus. A conical hopper with a porous mesh inner wall fluidizes the powder against the surface; mass flow rate is set by hopper height, not by valves that bridge and clog. Claimed throughput: at least 200 kg per hour, with the specification permitting up to 5,000.

The second patent corrects a problem the first creates downstream. When two calender rolls press a powder pile under 25 to 450 kilonewtons of force, the bearing blocks themselves deflect, and the gap measured at the bearings no longer reflects the actual distance between the roll surfaces. The patent assumes the measurement is wrong and corrects it through push and pull forces applied to non-corresponding bearings. Claimed accuracy: 30 microns or better, across the full force range.

Mass flow precision and gap precision are independent error sources, and the final electrode film thickness is sensitive to both. Tesla's January announcement was not a single discovery. It was the precision of two adjacent processes, multiplied together — the precision needed to make the 4680 cell at the volume and cost a sub-$30,000 Cybercab requires.

LFP dry electrode is not yet in production. Silicon-carbon anode chemistry is in development. Eggleston named the milestone "just the beginning." The patents make visible which beginning he meant.
